title: Dark Mode Is the Industry Standard. We Had to Make Premium Work in Light.
subtitle: Building a custom OKLCH color extraction API so Bibel TV users get a premium streaming experience that feels warm, not Hollywood
team: Design-led initiative with engineering partner, Berlin

hero_image.alt: Dynamic color extraction: same content artwork rendered with both light and dark mode themes

sections.headline: Dark Mode Is the Industry Standard. We Had to Make Premium Work in Light.
sections.subtext: Netflix, Spotify, Apple TV+ — every major streaming app defaults to dark mode. But Bibel TV's users — German faith communities — associate the brand with light, warmth, and hope. Building a premium streaming UX in light mode meant solving a problem the industry hasn't solved: perceptually-correct dynamic color extraction for both modes.

images.alt: Side-by-side comparison: same artwork with dark mode and light mode dynamic themes
images.caption: Same content. Two modes. One API.

sections.quote: You can't just invert dark-mode logic. OKLCH color extraction in light mode is a different problem entirely.
sections.context: The core design problem in one sentence

sections.headline: The Problem
sections.content:
Pray.com was launching in Germany — a premium faith app built by ex-Netflix/Spotify engineers with a dark-mode-first, globally-oriented product design. Bibel TV has more content and a stronger local brand, but a weaker app experience.

The brief we gave ourselves: be the free but premium alternative to Pray.com. That meant signaling quality without defaulting to dark mode. Bibel TV users actively *want* light mode — dark feels impersonal to them. It doesn't feel like Bibel TV.

Standard palette extraction (imgix, off-the-shelf libraries) works fine for dark mode. In light mode, the same artwork produces washed-out backgrounds, unreadable text, or colors that feel harsh. You need precise perceptual control over lightness and chroma — something RGB and HSL don't give you.

sections.headline: Why Standard Tools Don't Work
sections.content:
We started with imgix — it was already in our stack, and the palette extraction feature seemed like a quick win for dark mode. And it was. Dark mode looked great. But when user research revealed the light mode requirement, imgix couldn't handle it.

The problem: standard extraction algorithms optimize for contrast against dark backgrounds. Flip to light mode, and the same extracted palette creates insufficient contrast with text, overly aggressive primary colors, and background gradients that feel cheap.

We needed a system that could reason about *perceptual* color qualities — not just extract dominant pixel values.

sections.headline: The Decision: Custom OKLCH API
sections.content:
OKLCH is a perceptually uniform color space. You can reason about lightness (L) and chroma (C) in ways that RGB and HSL simply don't allow. It was the right tool for a problem that sits exactly at the intersection of design quality and technical infrastructure.

The decision tree looked like this:

**Off-the-shelf (imgix, libraries):** Fast, but light mode would be compromised. We'd have to default to dark mode — wrong for the brand.

**Custom OKLCH API:** More complexity upfront (K-means implementation, WCAG compliance, config management). But full control over both light and dark modes. Hero carousel with dynamic logo accent color as a signature effect. Full accessibility compliance built in.

I pitched the decision with a pragmatic approach: start with imgix (show dark mode working), build the custom extraction service in parallel, then present the upgrade as a natural fix for the problems user testing had surfaced. The upgrade sell was easy — "here's a working solution that fixes the exact problems you've seen."

steps.label: K-means Clustering
steps.description: Cluster image pixels to find dominant colors. We run K-means on the full image, not just a sample, because hero artwork quality matters.

steps.label: Perceptual Tuning
steps.description: Convert dominant colors to OKLCH. Extract base color (primary theme), two text colors (with WCAG contrast checking), background gradients, and a logo accent color for the hero carousel.

steps.label: WCAG Compliance Check
steps.description: Every text color is validated against WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios — 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text. If a color fails, the algorithm adjusts chroma and lightness until it passes.

steps.label: Live Config
steps.description: The API serves theme configs as live data. No code deploys needed. When artwork changes, colors update automatically.

steps.label: Figma Plugin Integration
steps.description: Built a companion TypeScript plugin so designers can test extracted palettes on artwork during the design phase, not after.

sections.headline: Technical Architecture
sections.content:
**Node.js service** — Lightweight API that receives an image URL and returns a theme config JSON.

**K-means clustering** — Custom implementation, tuned for perceptual color spaces rather than RGB pixel averaging.

**Output format:** { baseColor, textColors[2], backgroundGradient, logoAccentColor } — a complete theme specification.

**Live config** — Artwork updates trigger new color extraction. No code deploys, no app updates needed.

The Figma plugin is the designer's version of the same API: point it at artwork, get the palette. This means design and production use the same color logic.

sections.headline: Key Design Decisions
sections.content:
**Design-led infrastructure** — I owned the token API architecture, not just the Figma design. This is unusual for a Head of Design role. Infrastructure work typically lives with platform engineering. But color extraction *is* a design decision — and making it requires understanding both perceptual color science and the brand's emotional relationship with its users.

**Light mode first** — The entire extraction pipeline was optimized for light mode correctness first, then verified in dark mode. This is the reverse of every streaming app's standard approach.

**Hero accent as signature** — The logo's dynamic accent color (extracted from artwork and applied to the carousel header) became the app's most recognizable visual effect. It's a subtle detail but it bridges the premium streaming aesthetic with the warm, content-driven brand identity.

metrics.label: Light Mode Preference
metrics.value: 87% of users preferred light mode in validation surveys (n=40)

metrics.label: Premium Feel Rating
metrics.value: 4.2/5 — users rated the light-mode interface quality higher than dark-mode competitor benchmarks

metrics.label: WCAG Compliance
metrics.value: All extracted palettes pass WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios — no manual intervention needed

metrics.label: Delivery
metrics.value: API live and running. Android prototype validated. Ready for production integration.

sections.headline: What I Learned
sections.content:
The insight that matters: infrastructure work is design work. Most design leaders delegate token systems, color APIs, and automation to engineering. But the decisions embedded in these systems — perceptual color spaces, contrast compliance, multi-platform sync — are deeply design decisions. Owning them end-to-end means the final product matches the original vision.

The second insight: pitch the problem before the solution. I didn't lead with "we need a custom color API." I led with user research showing dark mode didn't work for our users, then presented the upgrade as a fix for that specific finding. The technical investment was justified by the user problem.

sections.headline: Why This Matters for Your Team
sections.content: If you're building a product in a category where industry defaults conflict with your brand identity, the answer isn't to copy the defaults. It's to invest in the technical depth needed to make your brand work. I built a color extraction API because the only alternative was shipping a product that felt wrong to our users. That level of ownership — from design direction through API architecture through WCAG compliance — is what design leadership looks like on a product that matters.

executive.summary: Built a custom OKLCH color extraction API so Bibel TV could deliver a premium streaming experience in light mode. 87% user preference for light mode, 4.2/5 premium rating, full WCAG AA compliance. Live and validated.
executive.angle: Design-led infrastructure investment that unlocked the brand's competitive differentiation

blog.summary: Every streaming app defaults to dark mode. We had to make it work in light — for users who want warmth, not Hollywood. Here's how.
blog.angle: The technical depth behind a design problem the industry hasn't solved

meta.source: Bibel TV project interview 2026-05-06 + comprehensive project case study
